locations.name: The Town Square
locations.short_desc: The heart of Brindlewick — a wide, grassy rectangle ringed by old maples, with a granite war memorial at its center.
locations.long_desc: The town square is where Brindlewick gathers and breathes. Four old sugar maples anchor its corners, their roots lifting the brick paths in gentle arcs. The granite war memorial stands at the center — four names in relief: William Hartwell, Joseph Finch, Thomas Grange, Lena Alderman. Someone has left a small bunch of wildflowers at its base; someone usually has. In summer, the square fills with tables for Founder's Day. In winter, a modest Christmas tree is lit here on the first Sunday of December, and the smell of cider drifts from thermoses. Benches face every direction. Pigeons, unhurried, own the eastern corner.
locations.history: The square has been the gathering place since 1830, when the first communal bonfire was held here to celebrate a successful harvest. Its current form — the brick paths, the memorial, the benches — was established in 1901 for the first Founder's Day.

time_variations.morning: A few people cut across the square on their way to work. The bakery smell drifts over from Millpond Row.
time_variations.afternoon: Children play near the benches after school. The pigeons investigate a dropped roll.
time_variations.evening: The square is quiet. Lights from the surrounding buildings warm the edges.
time_variations.night: The square is empty. The memorial is lit from below. A single lamp on the path.

seasonal_variations.spring: Crocuses push through the lawn around the memorial. The maples are budding, pale green.
seasonal_variations.summer: Full green shade. The Saturday farmers' market fills the brick paths with produce stands.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The maples turn first — deep orange, then copper. Leaves skitter across the brick paths.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the benches. The war memorial is frost-white. The Christmas tree stands at the center until January.

locations.name: Brindlewick Town Hall
locations.short_desc: A solid brick building from 1896, with a clocktower that has run exactly three minutes slow for over 150 years.
locations.long_desc: The Town Hall is the most substantial building in Brindlewick — two stories of dark red brick with tall windows and a white wooden clocktower that rises above the roofline like a polite suggestion of grandeur. The clock face is large and clearly readable from the square, its black hands at their perpetual three-minute offset. Mayor Windermere's office is on the second floor with a view of the lake. The ground floor holds the meeting room (open to the public), the town clerk's office, and a glass display case containing a scale model of Brindlewick as it appeared in 1900, made by a schoolchild who grew up to be an architect.
locations.history: Built 1896. The clocktower mechanism is original — installed by Cornelius Webb and set deliberately 3 minutes slow as a private tribute to Thomas Alderman. Every attempt to adjust it has resulted in it returning to 3 minutes slow within a week, which local engineers find baffling and locals find charming.

time_variations.morning: The town clerk, Vera Tibbs, is already at her desk. The building smells of old paper and floor wax.
time_variations.afternoon: Mayor Windermere's voice can sometimes be heard through the ceiling, on the phone.
time_variations.evening: Locked. The clocktower light is on.
time_variations.night: Dark and locked. The clocktower keeps going.

seasonal_variations.spring: The window boxes are freshly planted. The clock tower flag snaps in the spring wind.
seasonal_variations.summer: The maple out front throws deep shade over the entrance steps. Heat shimmers off the slate roof.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The ivy on the north wall has gone crimson. Fallen leaves collect against the front steps.
seasonal_variations.winter: Frost on the windowpanes. The clocktower bell sounds a little flatter in the cold air.

locations.name: The Clocktower
locations.short_desc: A narrow staircase leads to the clockwork chamber — tick-tock, tick-tock, three minutes behind the world.
locations.long_desc: The staircase to the clocktower is behind a door marked 'Maintenance — No Entry' that is never actually locked. Fourteen steep wooden steps bring you to a small chamber smelling of oil and old wood. The clockwork fills the room — brass gears, a slow swinging pendulum, the quiet authority of measured time. On the main gear housing, scratched in careful letters almost too small to read without leaning close: 'C.W. 1856 — for T.A. — 3 minutes to be kind.' The view from the clocktower window takes in the entire town and the full length of the lake.
locations.history: The mechanism was installed by Cornelius Webb in 1856 and reinstalled here when the Town Hall was rebuilt in 1896. The inscription has been here since 1856.
locations.unlock_condition: player has visited town_hall and has curiosity about the clock

seasonal_variations.spring: The view from the top is extraordinary in spring — the valley is every shade of green.
seasonal_variations.summer: The tower heats up in summer afternoons. Worth it for the view.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The best view of the foliage in all of Brindlewick. This is the place.
seasonal_variations.winter: Icy on the upper stairs. Be careful. The view is worth it.

time_variations.morning: The bell is loudest up here. Worth climbing at any hour.
time_variations.afternoon: The bell rings the quarter-hours.
time_variations.evening: The town below lights up as you watch.
time_variations.night: The view at night: the town glowing, the lake dark.

locations.name: Brindlewick Public Library
locations.short_desc: A warm, generous building on Millpond Row, smelling of old paper and lemon polish, with a fireplace that's lit nine months of the year.
locations.long_desc: The library occupies a converted Victorian house on Millpond Row, its facade made respectable with a carved wooden sign and window boxes that Juniper Salcedo tends with evident care. Inside: the reading room is everything. Tall windows face south and fill it with winter sun. The fireplace has a carved mantelpiece bearing the library's motto: 'Every question is welcome here.' Shelves line every wall, some original and dark with age. The reference section is Eleanor's domain — meticulous card catalogues alongside the digital terminals, because Eleanor believes both have merits. A back room houses the town archive: bound copies of every Chronicle going back to 1901, the original town records in acid-free boxes, and a locked glass case containing the most fragile documents.
locations.history: Built as a private house in 1888; purchased and converted for the library in 1899. First librarian was Dorothy Finch. The current head librarian, Eleanor Finch-Hartwell, is her great-great-niece.

time_variations.morning: Eleanor is shelving. Juni is cataloguing new donations at the front desk. The kettle is on.
time_variations.afternoon: Often has a few readers. After school, the study tables fill with children.
time_variations.evening: Open until 7pm weekdays. The fireplace is always lit after October.
time_variations.night: Closed. Dark windows. The porch light is on.

seasonal_variations.spring: The library garden out back is starting over — Eleanor's crocuses coming up around the bench.
seasonal_variations.summer: The reading garden behind the library is open. Roses in bloom against the south wall.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Woodsmoke drifts in whenever the door opens. The fireplace inside is lit from October through April.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the garden bench. Inside, the fireplace is going. The kettle is always on.

locations.name: The Library Archive Room
locations.short_desc: The back room of the library — cool, hushed, smelling of old paper and time.
locations.long_desc: Eleanor unlocks the archive room for researchers who ask politely and have a good reason. The room is cool and dim, its shelves packed with bound Chronicle volumes, deed boxes, census records, and the accumulated paper memory of 200 years. The locked glass case holds the most fragile items: an 1825 letter from Alderman Finch to a cousin describing 'a lake so clear you can see the bottom at twelve feet,' the original town charter with all signatures, and a small watercolor signed only 'M.F.' depicting the lake from the eastern shore with a girl sitting in the foreground. Juni has been trying to identify the artist for two years.

seasonal_variations.spring: Unchanged by season. The records go back to 1807.
seasonal_variations.summer: Cool and dim. A welcome retreat from the summer heat.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Eleanor lights a lamp in the afternoons now.
seasonal_variations.winter: Eleanor brings her own electric kettle. The room is habitable.

time_variations.morning: Eleanor opens it at 9, if she's in.
time_variations.afternoon: The best light for reading old documents.
time_variations.evening: Closed by 7.
time_variations.night: Dark and locked.

locations.name: The Copper Kettle Bakery
locations.short_desc: The warm center of the town's mornings — breads, pastries, a famous honey cake, and whatever Marigold felt like making today.
locations.long_desc: The smell reaches you half a block before you arrive: butter and yeast and something floral. The Copper Kettle occupies what was once a Victorian tearoom, and Marigold Osei has kept the original pressed-tin ceiling, the small round tables, and the etched glass on the door. The counter runs the length of the back wall, stacked with loaves and trays. The famous Brindlewick Honey Cake sits on its own glass stand near the register — a dense, golden slab with a particular floral sweetness that people have been trying to reproduce at home for fifteen years. Mari makes four a week; they always sell out.
locations.history: The building has operated as a bakery or tearoom since 1875. Mari Osei bought it in 2009 and 'made it what it was always trying to be,' in her own words.

time_variations.early_morning: Marigold's been here since 4. The first trays are coming out.
time_variations.morning: Busiest time. Queue sometimes reaches the door. Mari is behind the counter, flour-dusted, cheerful.
time_variations.afternoon: Quieter. Mari is often baking for tomorrow. A good time to actually talk.
time_variations.evening: Closed after 5pm. "A baker's day starts at 4am," Mari says.
time_variations.night: Dark and closed. The smell of bread lingers long after she's gone.

seasonal_variations.spring: The door is propped open with a brick. The smell of cardamom and butter drifts out into the mild air.
seasonal_variations.summer: Mari's summer menu is up — elderflower cakes and rhubarb jam. The display case sweats in the heat.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Apple and pear pastries have joined the usual things. The bakery smells like the whole season distilled.
seasonal_variations.winter: Steamed-up windows. The ginger cake is back. People linger here longer in winter, unwilling to go back out.

locations.name: Hartwell's General Store
locations.short_desc: The oldest continuous business in Brindlewick — groceries, hardware, peculiar sundries, and Harold Hartwell himself.
locations.long_desc: Hartwell's has been in some form on this site since James Hartwell set up his trading post in 1826. The current building dates from 1937. It sells everything from coffee beans to lamp oil, from rubber boots to birthday cards. The shelves have the comfortable chaos of a shop that has stocked itself by demand rather than plan. Harold Hartwell, 74, knows where everything is — including things you didn't know the store carried. He also knows most of the town's business, being married to the librarian and having watched three generations of families buy their groceries.
locations.history: Continuous Hartwell family operation since 1826, with gaps only during WWII (1942-45, when James Hartwell Jr. was serving).

seasonal_variations.spring: Seed packets and garden tools have appeared near the door. Mud tracked in from the parking lot.
seasonal_variations.summer: Sun hats and sunscreen in a bin by the entrance. Cold drinks in a cooler by the register.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Halloween candy, cider jugs, firewood bundles out front. The store smells of apples and candle wax.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow shovels lined up outside. Salt bags stacked by the door. The heat inside is a relief.

time_variations.morning: The store is organized, recently fronted. The coffee station is fresh.
time_variations.afternoon: Steady afternoon traffic. The deli counter is busy.
time_variations.evening: Open until 6. The last light is good for reading labels.
time_variations.night: Dark. The parking lot is empty. A sign gives the hours.

locations.name: The Millpond Diner
locations.short_desc: Booths, counter stools, good coffee, and the kind of breakfast that makes decisions easier.
locations.long_desc: The Millpond has been the town's informal meeting room for sixty years — the kind of place where deals are made over eggs and important conversations happen in corner booths. The coffee is strong. The pie is excellent. The walls are covered with photographs of the town across the decades, all unlabeled, which is a source of ongoing friendly dispute. Wally Chen, the owner and cook, has been here since 6am every day for 31 years. His daughter June does the front of house on weekdays.

seasonal_variations.spring: The window tables have a view of the millpond as it comes back to life — ducks returning, water moving again.
seasonal_variations.summer: The door is propped open in the afternoon. Ceiling fans spin. The lunch special is usually something cold.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Soup on the menu every day now. The windows fog up from the inside by mid-morning.
seasonal_variations.winter: The diner is one of the warmest spots in town. The coffee gets refilled without asking.

time_variations.early_morning: Wallace has the grill going. Early risers only. Coffee strong and immediate.
time_variations.morning: Breakfast rush. Farmers and contractors mostly. The counter is full.
time_variations.midday: Lunch crowd. All the tables are taken. The soup is good today.
time_variations.afternoon: The 2 o'clock lull. Wallace has a moment to breathe.
time_variations.evening: Dinner service. June Salazar works the floor. The specials board has been updated.
time_variations.night: Closed after 9. The OPEN sign off. A light still on in the kitchen.

locations.name: The Brindlewick Chronicle
locations.short_desc: The town's weekly newspaper — a slightly listing building whose noticeboard is read more religiously than any church bulletin.
locations.long_desc: The Chronicle building is narrow and three stories tall, which makes it look like it's been squeezed from both sides by the neighboring buildings. Inside: a printing area in the back (the original press is kept for display; it hasn't printed anything since 1968), a newsroom that is really one large desk covered in paper, and Oliver Peregrine's office upstairs, which smells of pipe tobacco despite the fact that Oliver quit smoking in 2008. The Chronicle comes out every Thursday. Its most-read feature is the anonymous 'Dear Neighbor' advice column.

seasonal_variations.spring: Deadline day is Tuesday. The press runs Wednesday morning.
seasonal_variations.summer: Summer edition has more photographs. Longer days, more events to cover.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The harvest fair issue is always the biggest of the year.
seasonal_variations.winter: Holiday listings, year-in-review. The year's back issues are bound and filed.

time_variations.morning: The editor is in early on deadline days.
time_variations.afternoon: Press day is Wednesday. Layout in the afternoon.
time_variations.evening: Quiet after 5. The press is in the back.
time_variations.night: Dark. The paper is printed early Wednesday morning.

locations.name: The Lantern Post Inn
locations.short_desc: The town's inn — warm, comfortable, smelling of woodsmoke and clean linen, with a porch that faces the lake.
locations.long_desc: The Lantern Post is a rambling Victorian house that has been accumulating additions since 1885. The original section has low ceilings and creaky floors; the 1985 addition has higher ceilings and a view of the water. Teddy Birch, 34, runs it with uncomplicated enthusiasm and a gift for making guests feel they've come home. The porch runs the full length of the original building, with rocking chairs and a view across Lake Street to the water. The common room has a fireplace, mismatched furniture that somehow all works together, and a shelf of books guests have left behind over decades.

seasonal_variations.spring: The inn's window boxes have been replanted. The front porch is being repainted.
seasonal_variations.summer: Full for the season. Guests on the porch in the evenings. The garden is well-maintained.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Leaf-peeper season. Bookings are tight from mid-September through October.
seasonal_variations.winter: Quiet. A few regulars. The common room fire is going day and night.

time_variations.early_morning: The kitchen is already going. The smell of coffee and breakfast.
time_variations.morning: Breakfast service. Guests checking out.
time_variations.afternoon: Check-in starts at 3. The porch is pleasant.
time_variations.evening: The common room is the heart of the inn. Guests gather.
time_variations.night: The night light is on. The door unlocked until midnight.

locations.name: The Wren & Whistle
locations.short_desc: The town's pub, open since 1851 — a low-ceilinged room with good ale, better food, and the most reliable gossip in Brindlewick.
locations.long_desc: The Wren & Whistle is the oldest building still in continuous original use in Brindlewick. The ceiling is low, the beams are original, and the floor is uneven in ways that become comforting once you know them. Clem Rourke occupies the corner stool most evenings. Barnaby Mossgrove comes in Tuesdays and Thursdays. The menu is simple and excellent — pies, soups, sandwiches, the occasional special. The cider is from Perkins. The beer comes from a small brewery two valleys over. The fireplace takes up most of one wall and is lit from October to April.

seasonal_variations.spring: The tavern's front window is open to the mild evening air. Pint glasses are cold again.
seasonal_variations.summer: The back garden is open through August. Picnic tables, lights in the trees, long evenings.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Dark beers are back on tap. A fire in the hearth. The place fills up earlier as the evenings shorten.
seasonal_variations.winter: The fire is going most nights. The windows glow amber through the frost. A particular warmth to it.

time_variations.morning: Dark, quiet, and locked. The pub smells faintly through the door.
time_variations.afternoon: The kitchen starts in the early afternoon. Open from 4.
time_variations.evening: Exactly what it should be. The bar is full by 7.
time_variations.night: Last call is announced without drama. People drift out slowly.

locations.name: Brindlewick Post Office
locations.short_desc: Small, efficient, and presided over by Artie Pryce, who has been delivering this town's mail since 1975.
locations.long_desc: The post office is the size of a large living room: a wall of PO boxes, a counter, a scale, and a shelf of packing supplies. Artie Pryce has been the postman here for 51 years. He knows every door in town, every household, and whose mail is interesting (he would never look, but he notices the postmarks). He is 76 and shows no sign of retiring. His delivery route takes him past every house in Brindlewick; he's the person most likely to notice if something is wrong or different.

seasonal_variations.spring: A hand-lettered sign in the window: "Packages may be muddy. We're all doing our best."
seasonal_variations.summer: The postmaster's fan oscillates on the counter. Long lines on package days.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Holiday shipping notices going up already. The waiting bench has seen better days.
seasonal_variations.winter: Holiday rush. Packages stacked to the ceiling. The smell of packing tape and stress.

time_variations.morning: Mail sorting in back. The counter opens at 9. A small queue.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon distribution is quiet. Package pickups.
time_variations.evening: Closed at 5. The drop box outside still works.
time_variations.night: Dark. The drop box is there if you need it.

locations.name: Finch Family Pharmacy
locations.short_desc: A proper old-fashioned pharmacy with a soda counter, run by Nola Finch with quiet competence.
locations.long_desc: The pharmacy has been a Finch family property since 1922, though Nola Finch (45) is only distantly related to the founding family. She keeps the original glass-fronted cabinets and the soda counter (still functional, though only used on busy summer Saturdays). The pharmacy section is at the back. Nola is competent, precise, and slightly tired of people asking whether she's related to the founders.

seasonal_variations.spring: Allergy medication prominently displayed up front. A tray of seeds by the register from the garden club.
seasonal_variations.summer: Sunscreen and bug spray near the door. The pharmacist has strong opinions about SPF.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Flu shot signs. Vitamin C by the register. People looking a little pre-emptively tired.
seasonal_variations.winter: Cold and flu remedies take over the first aisle. The pharmacist knows everyone by name and ailment.

time_variations.morning: Prescription pickups and the morning regulars. The pharmacist is thorough.
time_variations.afternoon: A slow midday. A good time to ask questions without a line.
time_variations.evening: Closing soon. Last calls for refills.
time_variations.night: Closed. The after-hours number is on the door.

locations.name: Alderman & Sons Hardware
locations.short_desc: No one named Alderman has worked here since 1987, but the sign stays because no one has the heart to change it.
locations.long_desc: Dale Putnam runs the hardware store; he bought it from the last Alderman to work retail in 1987 and kept the sign because it felt right. The store sells the tools and supplies that keep Brindlewick standing: lumber, paint, plumbing fittings, seeds, rope, the kind of specific thing you need when something breaks. Dale knows how to fix almost anything and will tell you how even if it means you don't buy anything.

seasonal_variations.spring: Garden stakes, seed-starting supplies, and new hose fittings near the front. Mud on every boot.
seasonal_variations.summer: Deck stain, outdoor paint, and screen repair supplies. The smell of sawdust and turpentine.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Storm prep supplies: weatherstripping, caulk, heat tape. People asking about pipe insulation.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice melt, snow shovels, and pipe warmers take over the front third of the store.

time_variations.morning: Contractors early. The staff knows what people need before they ask.
time_variations.afternoon: Weekend DIYers in the afternoon. Confused and well-meaning.
time_variations.evening: Closing out the register. Last customers.
time_variations.night: Closed. The loading dock is empty.

locations.name: Dr. Okafor's Medical Practice
locations.short_desc: A converted house on Maple Row that smells of antiseptic and, inexplicably, cardamom.
locations.long_desc: Dr. Ignatius Okafor's practice occupies the ground floor of a white clapboard house he also lives in — his apartment is upstairs. The waiting room has comfortable chairs, a fish tank, and magazines that are never more than three months old. Dr. Okafor is the only physician within 20 miles and sees everyone from newborns to centenarians. He is warm, precise, and has the quality of making patients feel that whatever they're dealing with is both perfectly manageable and worthy of his complete attention.

locations.name: Webb's Watch Repair
locations.short_desc: A small shop smelling of oil and metal, where Rosalind Webb fixes anything with moving parts.
locations.long_desc: The shop is barely wider than a hallway — a bench along the window, a wall of clocks, and Rosalind Webb bent over her work with a magnifying loupe. She fixes watches, clocks, music boxes, mechanical toys, and, reportedly, once fixed a music hall organ. The wall of clocks is a tourist attraction in itself: every one of them keeping different time except for one, the oldest, which runs three minutes slow. Rosalind will tell you it belonged to her great-grandfather if you ask.

seasonal_variations.spring: Spring cleaning of pocket watches. People bringing in things they found.
seasonal_variations.summer: A slow season. Roland is working on something complicated behind the counter.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Daylight saving brings in a rush of clocks that need adjusting.
seasonal_variations.winter: Holiday gifts: clocks, watches, things that need reconditioning.

time_variations.morning: Roland opens at 10. The shop is very quiet.
time_variations.afternoon: Appointments mostly. Walk-ins welcome.
time_variations.evening: Closed by 5.
time_variations.night: Dark. The clocks inside ticking on.

locations.name: The Old Mill Antiques
locations.short_desc: Three rooms of accumulated things, a stuck drawer that has never been opened, and the smell of a century's worth of history.
locations.long_desc: Miriam Thatcher runs the Old Mill Antiques out of a building that was once a grain merchant's warehouse. The shop has three rooms of furniture, objects, books, and curiosities accumulated over forty years. The famous stuck drawer is in a rolltop desk in the back room — Miriam has given up trying to open it and incorporated it into the shop's mythology. (The drawer is stuck because there's a clockwork mechanism inside, a small trinket box with a timer that will open on a specific date.) The shop smells of beeswax and old paper.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 10 Wednesday through Sunday.
time_variations.afternoon: The best time to browse. The owner knows the history of everything.
time_variations.evening: Closing by 5.
time_variations.night: Dark. A lamp left on near the front window.

locations.name: The Book Nook
locations.short_desc: Secondhand books in floor-to-ceiling shelves, a resident cat named Penelope, and a proprietor who reads every book before selling it.
locations.long_desc: The Book Nook is small and entirely full of books. Penelope, a gray tabby of indeterminate age, occupies whichever shelf she chooses and has never once been successfully relocated. Gus Fairweather has owned the shop since 1993 and reads every book before shelving it, which is why it takes him a while to restock. He makes careful handwritten recommendations that he tucks into the front covers. His section of local history books is the best in the valley after the library.

seasonal_variations.spring: New spring titles in the window. The used books section has been rearranged.
seasonal_variations.summer: Summer reading lists. Beach reads and long novels.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The best season for the book nook. Long books, shorter days.
seasonal_variations.winter: Warm in here. The cats sleep by the radiator. A good place to spend a cold afternoon.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 9. The cats are already awake.
time_variations.afternoon: The best time to browse. Fewer people.
time_variations.evening: Evening hours on Fridays. Reading group meets.
time_variations.night: Dark. The cats are elsewhere.

locations.name: Lakeside Park
locations.short_desc: A grassy park sloping to the boardwalk, with the statue of Alderman Finch, the lake glittering beyond.
locations.long_desc: Lakeside Park is the town's front yard — a gentle slope from Lake Street down to the boardwalk, with a dozen willows along the water's edge and the bronze statue of Alderman Finch standing in the center. The statue is life-sized, depicting Finch with one hand raised as if pointing at the lake. He has been pointing slightly more to the left than he was when installed. On summer evenings, this is where the town comes to sit. Families spread blankets. The teenagers congregate at the far willows. Older residents take the benches near the statue.

time_variations.morning: Dog walkers and early runners. The lake is misty in cool months.
time_variations.afternoon: Families, children. In summer, swimmers.
time_variations.evening: The most beautiful time — the light off the water, the willows moving.
time_variations.night: The park is quiet and dark. The lake sounds change at night.

seasonal_variations.spring: The park is coming back. Mud along the path edges, ducks on the water, and that particular smell of thaw.
seasonal_variations.summer: Peak season. Swimmers in the shallows, kayakers on the lake, families everywhere.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The trees are spectacular from the shoreline. Fewer people. The lake goes quiet.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the docks. The lake is frozen to within twenty feet of shore. Ice fishermen have set up near the point.

locations.name: The Lakefront Boardwalk
locations.short_desc: A wide wooden boardwalk along the lake's northern shore — the best place to watch the lake light on clear nights.
locations.long_desc: The boardwalk runs 600 yards along the lake's northern edge, from the park to the old boathouse. It was originally built in 1989 and restored in 2015; the new planks still smell faintly of cedar when it rains. Benches face the water at intervals. At night, on clear evenings especially, the view south across the lake is extraordinary — the mountains reflected, the two islands dark shapes in the silver water. From the southern-facing section of the boardwalk, due south, you can sometimes see the lake light: faint, pale blue-green, trembling just below the surface.

time_variations.morning: A few early walkers. The lake is misty. Herons on the pilings.
time_variations.afternoon: Busy in summer — families, ice cream, the whole thing.
time_variations.evening: The best time. Locals walk here after dinner. The lake light is visible on clear nights from the southern stretch.
time_variations.night: The boardwalk is lit by small lantern-post lights. The lake light is visible at its best around midnight on very clear nights.

seasonal_variations.spring: The boardwalk creaks in the spring wind. Ice-out was two weeks ago. The water is still gray-green and cold.
seasonal_variations.summer: The best evenings of the year happen here. Light on the water, loons calling, people walking.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The light on the water is extraordinary in October. Cold enough for a jacket by seven o'clock.
seasonal_variations.winter: The lake ice extends to within arm's reach of the dock. The lanterns are on, but few people come out this late.

locations.name: The Lake Pier
locations.short_desc: An old wooden pier extending sixty feet into the lake — fishing in the morning, watching the stars at night.
locations.long_desc: The pier has been here in some form since the 1840s. The current structure dates from 1971. It extends sixty feet into the lake, with a wider platform at the end and two benches worn smooth by decades of sitting. Fishing is permitted and practiced. The water is clear enough here to see the bottom for a good fifteen feet. At the end of the pier, looking south, you are directly north of where the lake light appears.

seasonal_variations.spring: The pier has been re-planked after winter. Fresh wood, new rope on the cleats.
seasonal_variations.summer: Busy. Fishermen at dawn, swimmers at noon, families at sunset.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Nearly empty. The best fishing of the year, the regulars say.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice. The pier is closed at the near end by a chain. The view from the edge is remarkable.

time_variations.early_morning: Fishermen are here before sunrise. No conversation required.
time_variations.morning: The pier gets busy as the morning warms up.
time_variations.afternoon: Families and swimmers in summer. Quieter in other seasons.
time_variations.evening: The best light. People take photographs.
time_variations.night: The pier is empty. The water sounds louder.

locations.name: Mira's Boat Rental & Bait
locations.short_desc: Rowboats, canoes, fishing gear, and Sadie Mirabel, who knows the lake better than anyone alive.
locations.long_desc: The little yellow building at the end of the boardwalk has offered boat rentals since 1962, when the first Sadie Mirabel (grandmother of the current one) started it. The name has always been 'Mira's' — a play on 'Mirrowell' and, perhaps, something else. The current Sadie Mirabel, 58, runs the boats, sells bait, and is the person to ask about the lake: its moods, its currents, its depths, and, if you're patient and have earned a bit of her trust, what she thinks is down there in the deep water due south.

seasonal_variations.spring: The boats are back in the water after winter storage. Mira is repainting the dock cleats.
seasonal_variations.summer: Busiest time. Rentals go quickly on weekends. The bait cooler is well-stocked.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Quiet weeks. Mira offers a discount after Labor Day. The foliage from the water is unbeatable.
seasonal_variations.winter: Closed for the season. A hand-lettered sign: "Back in May. Thank you for another good summer."

time_variations.early_morning: Mira is rigging the boats before the first customers arrive.
time_variations.morning: First rentals of the day. Mira gives the safety talk quickly but thoroughly.
time_variations.afternoon: Returns and re-rentals. The dock is active.
time_variations.evening: Boats coming in. Mira checks them off. Closing by 6.
time_variations.night: Locked and dark. The boats tied at the dock.

locations.name: The Old Alderman Boathouse
locations.short_desc: A weathered structure at the end of the boardwalk — locked, slightly leaning, full of something.
locations.long_desc: The Alderman family boathouse dates from 1872, replacing the one lost in the 1879 flood. It has been unused for at least forty years. The door is padlocked. Through the gaps in the boards you can see old boats, rusted equipment, and what appears to be a considerable collection of the Alderman family's storage. Constance Alderman has a key. She says she keeps meaning to sort through it.

seasonal_variations.spring: The boathouse timbers still hold, but barely. Swallows have nested in the rafters again.
seasonal_variations.summer: Dark and cool inside, even on hot days. The smell of old wood and lake water.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The season has turned. Spiderwebs across the doorway. Leaves drifting in through the gaps in the boards.
seasonal_variations.winter: The ice has crept to the edge of the boathouse pilings. The old rope inside is frozen stiff.

locations.name: The Alderman Estate
locations.short_desc: The largest, oldest house in Brindlewick — a Victorian pile set back from the road behind iron gates, where Constance Alderman has lived for 85 years.
locations.long_desc: The Alderman Estate is a three-story Victorian house in good repair but clearly old — wide porches, shuttered upper windows, a garden that is large and well-tended but somehow private. The iron gate at the road is never locked but feels private enough that people generally don't walk up the drive without being invited. Constance Alderman, 85, lives here with her housekeeper, Dot Flowers, and a remarkable number of cats. The east wing, according to what Eleanor found in the town records, contains a sealed room that has been walled off since 1866.

seasonal_variations.spring: The overgrown garden is doing what it wants. Daffodils come back every year no matter what.
seasonal_variations.summer: The estate is as grand as it ever gets — the trees in full leaf, the lawn long and meadow-like.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The copper beeches turn last — a deep, burning red in late October. Hard to look away.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the stone balustrade. The house looks like a Thomas Cole painting. Forbidding and beautiful.

time_variations.morning: The estate is quiet in the morning. Birdsong and the distant sound of the lake.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon light is hard on the old facade.
time_variations.evening: The estate looks best at dusk — the windows catch the light.
time_variations.night: Dark and quiet. The estate keeps its secrets.

locations.name: Alderman Finch's Study
locations.short_desc: A sealed room untouched since 1866 — the founder's desk, his papers, a particular watercolor on the wall.
locations.long_desc: The room is small — a private study, twelve feet square, with a fireplace that hasn't been lit in 160 years. The desk is covered in papers, preserved by the cold and the sealed air. Bookshelves. An oil lamp. On the wall above the desk, a watercolor of the lake at dawn — signed, in the corner, 'M.F.' The founder's diary is in the top desk drawer. It contains the full history of the town's founding, including the true account of Mira Finch, who named the lake and was written out of the family record.

seasonal_variations.spring: The air in here is unchanged by season. Old, still, and cool.
seasonal_variations.summer: Cool even in summer. The documents are safer for it.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The light through the hidden window changes angle. An hour of direct sun in October.
seasonal_variations.winter: The coldest room in the estate, but not unpleasantly so. A thick quiet.

time_variations.morning: The room is unchanged.
time_variations.afternoon: A shaft of afternoon light through the upper window.
time_variations.evening: The room darkens quickly.
time_variations.night: Complete darkness. You wouldn't want to be here without a light.

locations.name: St. Agatha's Chapel
locations.short_desc: The oldest standing building in Brindlewick, built by every family in the valley in a single summer in 1831.
locations.long_desc: The chapel is small and white, with a pointed steeple and a bell that still rings on Sundays. The interior is simple: oak pews worn smooth, plain windows (one stained-glass addition from 1912, depicting the lake at sunrise), a stone font. The register of baptisms, marriages, and burials going back to 1831 is kept in a locked box behind the altar. Reverend Prewitt, who is 71 and has been the minister here for 30 years, knows the register by heart. A wooden door at the rear of the chapel leads out to the churchyard.

seasonal_variations.spring: Lilies along the chapel path, just planted. Easter has recently passed; the door stands open.
seasonal_variations.summer: The chapel garden is at its best. Bees in the lavender. The interior is cool and still.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Harvest decorations on the chapel steps — gourds, dried corn, chrysanthemums.
seasonal_variations.winter: Advent candles in the windows. The interior glows. Even people who don't attend services sometimes stop to look.

time_variations.morning: Morning service on Sundays. Quiet the rest of the week. The door is usually open.
time_variations.afternoon: Often empty. The light through the windows is remarkable in the afternoon.
time_variations.evening: Vespers on Wednesdays. Otherwise quiet.
time_variations.night: Dark. The exterior lantern is lit. The door is locked.

locations.name: Brindlewick Cemetery
locations.short_desc: Behind the chapel — generations of Brindlewick families, their stones moss-covered and familiar.
locations.long_desc: The cemetery is behind the chapel, separated by a low stone wall. The oldest stones are nearest the chapel wall, worn nearly smooth by weather. The Finch and Alderman plots are the largest. Among the stones, if you look carefully, is one that stands slightly apart from the others, near the back wall — smaller, plainly inscribed: 'Mira Finch, 1815-1878. She named the lake.' Someone has placed it here; it wasn't here in the 1960s. No one knows who put it.
locations.address: Behind St. Agatha's Chapel, Maple Row

seasonal_variations.spring: The grave tenders have been through. Fresh flowers on some of the older stones.
seasonal_variations.summer: The grass is well-kept. The old willows in full leaf. A quiet, shaded place.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Leaves have drifted against the older stones. The names are still legible on most.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow has softened the hard geometry of the headstones. The ironwork fence is frosted.

time_variations.morning: The groundskeeper starts early. Quiet and orderly.
time_variations.afternoon: The most commonly visited time.
time_variations.evening: Fewer people. The light is long in the late afternoon.
time_variations.night: Closed at dusk. Gates locked.

locations.name: The Founders' Graves
locations.short_desc: An older cemetery on the lower slope of Copper Hill — a dozen stones, the oldest in town.
locations.long_desc: Before the main cemetery was established in 1845, the earliest settlers buried their dead on the lower slope of Copper Hill. There are twelve graves here, in a small enclosure of old stone walls. The two Alderman founders, the first Hartwell, three Finches. The stones are old enough that some are hard to read. Thomas Alderman's stone, the largest, has an inscription: 'He helped. When in doubt, he helped.' The old water tower is visible a short walk up the slope.

seasonal_variations.spring: Someone has left early violets on Alderman Finch's stone. It happens every year.
seasonal_variations.summer: The oldest section is overgrown at the edges. The stones are warm in the afternoon sun.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Fallen leaves cover some of the lowest stones. The names still show through.
seasonal_variations.winter: The stones are frosted clean. The carved dates are easier to read in the low winter light.

time_variations.morning: Quiet and undisturbed.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon light reads the stone clearly.
time_variations.evening: Long shadows across the old graves.
time_variations.night: The cemetery is closed. This corner especially dark.

locations.name: Brindlewick Elementary School
locations.short_desc: A brick building from 1949, still using the original schoolhouse bell from 1836.
locations.long_desc: The elementary school is a solid postwar building with a large playground and the kind of institutional smell (pencil shavings, floor cleaner) that is somehow comforting. It houses grades K-8; high schoolers travel by bus to the regional school two towns over. Petra Holloway teaches science here. The original schoolhouse bell — bronze, twelve inches across, dated 1836 — hangs in a frame in the front corridor.

seasonal_variations.spring: Paper tulips in the windows. Mud tracked in despite the mat. The playground has come back to life.
seasonal_variations.summer: Closed for summer. The yard is empty. The swings move in the wind.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Back in session. Lunch smells drifting from the kitchen by 11. Bus line in the morning.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow day art in the windows. The parking lot has been plowed into ridges.

time_variations.morning: Arrival time. Buses, cars, children.
time_variations.afternoon: End-of-day dismissal at 2:45. Buses and pickups.
time_variations.evening: Parent meetings some evenings.
time_variations.night: Dark. The playground empty.

locations.name: The Garden Club Greenhouse
locations.short_desc: A glass-and-steel greenhouse where the Garden Club grows everything that goes into the town's common planters.
locations.long_desc: The Garden Club greenhouse is larger than you'd expect — a proper commercial-sized structure behind the school. It's open to visitors on Saturday mornings. Inside: rows of seedlings, overwintering tropicals, a legendary collection of begonias, and the perennial low-level debate about whether a particular rose cultivar counts as a hybrid tea. Dorothy Alderman (Constance's niece) runs the club with mild tyranny and considerable expertise.

seasonal_variations.spring: This is the greenhouse's moment — seedlings for the whole town, heat lamps on, the smell of soil and possibility.
seasonal_variations.summer: Full production. Tomatoes starting to redden. The greenhouse is warm even when the door is open.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Putting things to bed for winter. Bulb sales outside. The smell of woodsmoke from somewhere nearby.
seasonal_variations.winter: Nearly empty inside. A few overwintering geraniums, frost on the glass. But warm.

time_variations.morning: Members arrive early. The greenhouse is warm and busy by 9.
time_variations.afternoon: Planting and tending. A good time to ask questions.
time_variations.evening: Closing. The hoses rolled up.
time_variations.night: The grow lights on inside. The greenhouse glows.

locations.name: The Sheriff's Office
locations.short_desc: Brindlewick's law enforcement occupies a single office with a coffee maker, a coat rack, and a bulletin board of mildly vexing situations.
locations.long_desc: Sheriff Levi Quartermaine runs the most peaceable police operation in the county — mostly parking, the occasional noise complaint, and a quarterly visit from a raccoon that has worked out how to open the dumpster behind the diner. The bulletin board holds: three missing cats, one lost bicycle (a child's, left against the library fence), and a handwritten note that says 'The statue has moved again (est. 1/8 inch north, this spring). Monitoring continues.' Levi has been monitoring the statue for seven years.

seasonal_variations.spring: The sheriff's cruiser has been washed for the first time since October. A good sign.
seasonal_variations.summer: The office windows are open. Slow season, more or less. Minor things.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Hunting season paperwork. The bulletin board has the usual flyers.
seasonal_variations.winter: The heat is turned up high. Someone has left a plate of cookies on the counter.

time_variations.morning: The morning shift. Coffee, paperwork.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon is the busiest for walk-ins.
time_variations.evening: The night deputy takes over at 7.
time_variations.night: Someone is always here. The radio crackles occasionally.

locations.name: Brindlewick Fire Station
locations.short_desc: A volunteer fire department that mostly handles chimney fires and floods the square's ice rink in winter.
locations.long_desc: The fire station has two bays — a modern pumper truck and a 1968 ladder truck that is kept running by the combined will of the volunteer department and Barnaby Mossgrove, who can fix anything. The department is all-volunteer, and most of the town is on the roster. Their biggest annual event is not a fire but the Winter Market, which they organize and host in the station's parking lot.

seasonal_variations.spring: The bay doors are open. The truck is being washed. A good-natured argument happening about coffee.
seasonal_variations.summer: Community barbecue in the parking lot on the first Saturday of August. The station is spotless.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Fall brush burning permits posted in the window. The shift changes at 6.
seasonal_variations.winter: Both trucks ready. Extra sand in the lot. The duty roster is posted beside the door.

time_variations.morning: Shift change at 7. The equipment check is already underway.
time_variations.afternoon: Training sometimes in the parking lot.
time_variations.evening: Night crew coming on.
time_variations.night: The station is lit. Someone is always awake.

locations.name: Perkins Cider House
locations.short_desc: On the Alderman Road, half a mile out of town — Agnes Perkins's cider operation, her apple orchard, and the source of a particular ingredient.
locations.long_desc: The Perkins property is a working farm — orchard, chicken yard, vegetable garden, and the cider barn where Agnes makes her famous dry cider and, in good years, a limited run of something she calls her 'elder blend.' The walk out here is pleasant: the Alderman Road follows the creek for a bit before curving into the hills. Agnes, 71, is the fourth generation to run the place and the most opinionated. She and Constance Alderman have kept up the Alderman-Perkins feud as a sort of performance art.
locations.address: Alderman Road, ½ mile from Millpond Row

seasonal_variations.spring: Quiet. Aging barrels inside. The tasting room won't open until summer.
seasonal_variations.summer: Fresh-pressed cider available weekends. The bees are a problem near the press.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Peak season. Pressing runs every weekend. The whole property smells of apples.
seasonal_variations.winter: Closed tasting room, but hard cider is available at Hartwell's year-round.

time_variations.morning: Closed. The cider press is sometimes audible from the road on Thursday mornings.
time_variations.afternoon: Tasting room open Thursday through Sunday from 11.
time_variations.evening: The tasting room closes at 6. Outdoor seating if the weather allows.
time_variations.night: Dark. The orchard is quiet.

locations.name: The Covered Bridge
locations.short_desc: A covered wooden bridge over Heron's Creek on the north road, with a hidden inscription on its interior beam.
locations.long_desc: The covered bridge spans Heron's Creek on the north road out of town — a classic New England structure, red-painted, about 40 feet long. The interior smells of old wood and creek water. The bridge was built in 1842 and has been repaired many times. On the main upper beam, in the interior, in small careful letters that require looking up and leaning close: 'M.F. was here — 1833. The lake is hers.' It is carved, not painted, and is the oldest inscription in town. No one had noticed it until Juni Salcedo discovered it in 2021 while photographing the bridge for a restoration grant application.
locations.address: Old Mill Road over Heron's Creek

seasonal_variations.spring: The stream below is running fast with snowmelt, brown and loud under the bridge.
seasonal_variations.summer: The bridge planks are warm underfoot. The water below is low and clear.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The most photographed spot in town. Foliage frames the view from both ends.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice on the planks. The stream below is partly frozen. The bridge timbers creak in the cold.

time_variations.morning: The light comes through the gaps in the boards. The stream is loud in spring.
time_variations.afternoon: A popular stop for walkers. The shaded interior is cool.
time_variations.evening: The light at the far end makes a frame of the water.
time_variations.night: Dark inside the bridge. The stream sound amplified under the roof.

locations.name: The Old Mill Ruins
locations.short_desc: The stone foundations and one standing wall of the mill from 1862, beside Heron's Creek.
locations.long_desc: The old grist mill operated until 1902 and was largely dismantled in 1930. What remains is the stone foundation, one gable end wall standing to full height, and the millrace — the channel that directed the creek into the wheel. The millrace is full of watercress and small trout. The standing wall has a faded painted advertisement on it: BRINDLEWICK MILLS — FINEST FLOUR IN THE VALLEY, the letters just legible in the right light. A path follows the creek bank north toward the covered bridge.

seasonal_variations.spring: The ruins are half-hidden by new growth — ferns pushing up through the stonework.
seasonal_variations.summer: Deep shade here. Swallows nest in the upper walls. The stream is quiet.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The ruins look their most photogenic in October — stone and leaf.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow fills the window openings. The stonework is beautiful in its ruined clarity.

time_variations.morning: The birds are loudest here in the morning.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon light comes through the upper stones.
time_variations.evening: The ruins are most atmospheric at dusk.
time_variations.night: Dark. The stream sounds different at night.

locations.name: The Water Tower
locations.short_desc: The old water tower on the lower slope of Copper Hill, painted white with 'BRINDLEWICK' in faded red.
locations.long_desc: The water tower dates from 1934, when the town's first pressurized water system was installed. It was decommissioned when a new system was built in 1989 but kept for its historical value and because 'BRINDLEWICK' in 18-inch red letters is visible from the main road and serves as a welcome sign. From its base you have a view of the town's full layout — the streets, the square, the library, the inn — and the lake beyond. The old founders' burial ground is a short walk down the slope.

seasonal_variations.spring: The town's name is freshly repainted on the tank. Spring maintenance.
seasonal_variations.summer: The shadow of the tower tracks across the field in a long arc through the afternoon.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The tower is visible above the trees even after the leaves have gone.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice on the ladder rungs. The tank is insulated but you wouldn't want to check.

time_variations.morning: The early light hits the tank first.
time_variations.afternoon: The shadow of the tower reaches east by 3.
time_variations.evening: The tank is painted in last light.
time_variations.night: A red warning light blinks at the top.

locations.name: The Brindlewick Lighthouse
locations.short_desc: A small lighthouse at the lake's edge, built in 1882 — it has never warned anyone away from anything, but the light is beautiful.
locations.long_desc: The lighthouse is only 30 feet tall, which makes it the least imposing lighthouse in New England. It was built as a memorial after the 1879 flood and as a practical guide for fishing boats returning after dark. The light — automated since 1957 — is visible across the full length of the lake. A small door at the base leads to a circular staircase. The keeper's log is stored in the library; the last entry by a human keeper is dated November 14, 1957.

seasonal_variations.spring: The lighthouse keeper's cottage is freshly painted. The light turns again after winter maintenance.
seasonal_variations.summer: The lighthouse is at its most beautiful at sunset, the beam visible for miles.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Fog in the evenings. The foghorn sounds twice before dawn. You can hear it all over town.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice at the base of the lighthouse. The beam sweeps through the snow.

time_variations.morning: The lighthouse keeper begins the day's maintenance early.
time_variations.afternoon: The lighthouse is most visible from the lake on clear afternoons.
time_variations.evening: The light comes on at dusk, precisely.
time_variations.night: The beam sweeps. You can see it from the inn.

locations.name: The Brindlewick Trailhead
locations.short_desc: The start of the mountain trail system — a wooden signpost and a trail box with maps and a pencil stub.
locations.long_desc: The trailhead is on the north side of town where the houses thin out and the trees begin. A large wooden signpost indicates distances: The Lookout (2.1 mi), Copper Hill Summit (3.4 mi), Heron's Creek Loop (1.8 mi). A weatherproof box holds trail maps — always slightly damp, pencil stub attached on a string. A register book inside has the handwritten entries of everyone who's gone up: dates, names, notes. The earliest entry is from 1978.

seasonal_variations.spring: Mud season. The trail register is back out. First hikers leaving boot prints in the soft ground.
seasonal_variations.summer: The trailhead is busy. Parking is tight on weekends. The trail is dry and well-maintained.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Peak hiking season. The summit views are extraordinary. Foliage from the lookout is worth the climb.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snowshoes required above 1,200 feet. The register notes are fewer but more sincere.

time_variations.morning: Early hikers. Cars in the lot before 7.
time_variations.afternoon: Busiest. Day hikers returning and departing.
time_variations.evening: The late afternoon crowd coming back down.
time_variations.night: The trailhead is dark. The lot is empty.

locations.name: The Lookout
locations.short_desc: A cleared rocky ledge at 2.1 miles — the best view of Brindlewick and the valley, the lake spread below you.
locations.long_desc: The trail opens suddenly onto a broad rocky ledge, and the view is worth every step: the full valley below, the town a cluster of white and red rooftops around the green square, the lake a long silver shape extending southward, the farms and orchards of the outskirts, and beyond them, more mountains. On clear days you can see 40 miles. There is a single bench here, installed in 1989 by the Garden Club, with a small bronze plate: 'In memory of Dorothy Webb, who came up here every birthday.'

seasonal_variations.spring: The valley is patchwork — bare trees, green beginnings, the lake still gray.
seasonal_variations.summer: The best views of the lake from up here. Green in every direction. Worth the walk.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The reason people come to Brindlewick. The whole valley turns from up here — amber, red, copper.
seasonal_variations.winter: The valley is white. The lake is frozen. The silence up here is absolute.

time_variations.morning: The valley is in morning haze. Worth arriving early for.
time_variations.afternoon: Clear views on good days. The lake glitters.
time_variations.evening: The light is extraordinary at dusk from up here.
time_variations.night: The lights of the village visible below. Stars unobstructed.

locations.name: The Warden's Cabin
locations.short_desc: The forest warden's cabin at the edge of the state forest — smoke from the chimney, a stack of firewood, a friendly dog.
locations.long_desc: Clem Rourke (55) lives here year-round, having transitioned from full-time warden to part-time when he semi-retired. The cabin is warm, cluttered with field guides and hiking maps, and inhabited by a large dog named Biscuit. Clem knows every trail in the system, every bird that nests in the valley, and has strong opinions about the lake. He is the person Fletcher Grange usually calls when something odd is happening in the natural world.

seasonal_variations.spring: The warden has been back since March. Smoke from the chimney, lights on inside.
seasonal_variations.summer: The cabin is usually empty during the day — the warden is out on the trail.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The wood pile is built up. Preparations for winter underway.
seasonal_variations.winter: The cabin is occupied more consistently in winter. The warden is the only human presence on the ridge.

time_variations.morning: Smoke from the chimney. The warden sets out early.
time_variations.afternoon: Usually empty — warden on the trail.
time_variations.evening: The cabin is lit in the evenings. A warm square of yellow.
time_variations.night: The cabin is dark by 10. The ridge is quiet.

locations.name: The Finch Family Orchard
locations.short_desc: Twenty-odd acres of apple trees on the south-facing slope of the north hill — not officially open to the public, but no one's ever been turned away.
locations.long_desc: The Finch orchard occupies the south-facing lower slope of the hill above town — sheltered from the north, angled toward the afternoon sun, which is why the apples have thrived here since 1856. Eleanor's family still owns it but leases the working operation to a neighboring farmer. In October it's extraordinary — the air sweet, the light low and golden, every tree loaded. A stone wall marks the eastern boundary. On the interior of the gate post, carved in 1856: 'A.F. planted the first tree here.' Nobody is sure who A.F. was.

seasonal_variations.spring: Apple blossoms. The whole orchard white and humming with bees.
seasonal_variations.summer: Green apples, still months from harvest. The tall grass between the rows needs cutting.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Harvest. Crates stacked, ladders propped in trees, the smell of ferment at the edges.
seasonal_variations.winter: The bare trees are pruned and stark. Snow in the furrows. The orchard is patient.

time_variations.morning: The orchard in morning light, dew on the grass.
time_variations.afternoon: Warm in summer afternoons. The smell of ripening fruit.
time_variations.evening: The light through the apple trees at dusk.
time_variations.night: Dark and still. The trees are patient.

locations.name: The Millpond
locations.short_desc: A broad, still section of Heron's Creek where the old mill backed up the water — now a duck pond, beloved and slow.
locations.long_desc: The millpond is a remnant of the old mill's operation — the original dam is still in place, though much reduced. The water is still and shallow, full of rushes and ducks. A wooden footbridge crosses the creek just above the pond. In summer this is a popular spot for children. In winter it freezes reliably enough for skating, and the fire department brings a small warming hut.

seasonal_variations.spring: Ice-out was last week. The pond surface is restless, rearranging itself in the wind.
seasonal_variations.summer: Still and green. Lily pads at the south end. Frogs in the evening.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Leaf litter on the surface. The pond mirrors the trees better than it should.
seasonal_variations.winter: Frozen solid. Ice fishermen have drilled holes near the far shore.

time_variations.morning: Mist on the pond in cool months. Ducks moving.
time_variations.afternoon: The pond reflects everything clearly in calm weather.
time_variations.evening: The best light. The pond turns colors.
time_variations.night: The pond is dark and quiet. Frogs in summer.

locations.name: The Community Hall
locations.short_desc: A large barn-style building where the town gathers for events — Harvest Fair prep, Winter Market planning, and the monthly potluck.
locations.long_desc: The Community Hall was built in 1956 by the combined effort of a dozen families who wanted a large indoor space for events that wouldn't require renting anything. It has a proper stage, a commercial kitchen, folding tables and chairs enough for 300, and a mounted moose head above the door that has been there as long as anyone can remember and that no one has claimed responsibility for. Every major town event organizes here.

seasonal_variations.spring: The town meeting schedule is posted. Mud on the entry mat. Folding chairs still in use.
seasonal_variations.summer: Community theater runs through August. The stage entrance is around back.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The harvest fair is held here. Canning competition, bake-off, town records going back decades.
seasonal_variations.winter: Caroling here on the twentieth. The hall is warm and the acoustics are good.

time_variations.morning: Setup for whatever is happening today.
time_variations.afternoon: Meetings, events, or just the caretaker cleaning up.
time_variations.evening: Most events are in the evenings. The parking lot fills.
time_variations.night: Dark. The fire doors alarmed.

locations.name: The Grange Hall
locations.short_desc: The farmers' organization hall on the north road — older than most buildings in town, used for the Harvest Fair committee meetings.
locations.long_desc: The Grange Hall is a 1910 building that smells of old wood and agricultural history. It's used primarily for the Grange's own monthly meetings, Harvest Fair planning, and as a storage facility for fair supplies. Fletcher Grange (no relation to the organization; the name confusion has been going on for 60 years) is on the board.

seasonal_variations.spring: The seed and equipment share program is running. Farmers in and out all morning.
seasonal_variations.summer: Quieter than the rest of the year. The big events are over; harvest hasn't started.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The grange is at its most important. Harvest tallies, preservation workshops, the apple pressing.
seasonal_variations.winter: Monthly meetings, a woodworking group on Thursdays, and not much else.

time_variations.morning: The hall opens at 9 on activity days. Farmers mostly.
time_variations.afternoon: Quieter afternoons, except during harvest.
time_variations.evening: Evening meetings and events.
time_variations.night: Dark. The parking lot empty.

locations.name: The Woolen Bear
locations.short_desc: Yarn, knitwear, and knitting instruction from Margaret Hollis, who has been knitting since age 6.
locations.long_desc: The shop is warm and softly colored — walls of yarn in every weight and hue, finished garments on display, a spinning wheel in the window that sometimes turns. Margaret Hollis, 52, runs the shop and offers knitting classes that are perpetually oversubscribed. The shop cat is an orange tabby named Butter.

seasonal_variations.spring: New dye lots in — spring colors. Light wool, cotton-blend yarns.
seasonal_variations.summer: Quieter season. Clara uses the time to restock and dye new skeins.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The wool shop comes alive. Heavy yarns out, thick needles, everyone starting something new.
seasonal_variations.winter: The most popular time of year. Knitting groups take over the back corner every Saturday.

time_variations.morning: Clara opens at 10. The shop is freshly tidied.
time_variations.afternoon: The knitting circle meets Tuesday afternoons.
time_variations.evening: Closed by 5 most days.
time_variations.night: Dark. The yarn in the window.

locations.name: Brindlewick Pottery Studio
locations.short_desc: Sylvie Tran's studio — wheel-thrown pots, the smell of clay, and someone who notices everything.
locations.long_desc: The pottery studio occupies a converted outbuilding behind the Finch Lane houses. The front section is the shop; the back is the working studio, visible through a window. Sylvie Tran, 33, makes functional, beautiful pottery — bowls, mugs, serving dishes — that she sells here and at a few shops in the city. She is observant in the manner of someone who spends long hours looking at the shapes of things, and she notices things about people that they haven't said.

seasonal_variations.spring: The studio doors are open. New pieces drying on the racks outside.
seasonal_variations.summer: The kiln runs more in summer — more visitors, more orders.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The studio is warm from the kiln, a good thing on cool days.
seasonal_variations.winter: The kiln keeps the whole studio warm. It's one of the cozier spots in town.

time_variations.morning: The wheel is already going when the shop opens.
time_variations.afternoon: Classes in the afternoon.
time_variations.evening: Evening wheels available on appointment.
time_variations.night: The kiln sometimes runs overnight. A faint warmth from the building.

locations.name: Copper Hill Gallery
locations.short_desc: A small gallery showing local artists, with a permanent display of historical photographs of Brindlewick.
locations.long_desc: The gallery is run by a loose cooperative of local artists; Petra Holloway volunteers as coordinator. The walls rotate with new work. The permanent display is at the back: a timeline of photographs of Brindlewick from the 1890s onward, carefully labeled where labels are possible and annotated with question marks where they aren't. Several people in the photographs are unidentified.

seasonal_variations.spring: New show just hung. Watercolors and oils, mostly local subjects.
seasonal_variations.summer: Open for the tourist season. Two visiting artists in residence.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The foliage show is a Brindlewick tradition. The gallery fills with leaf work, landscapes, plein air.
seasonal_variations.winter: Quieter, but the permanent collection is always worth seeing.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 11. The lighting is carefully set.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon light changes the work.
time_variations.evening: Openings are in the evening.
time_variations.night: Dark. The artwork invisible.

locations.name: The Ice Cream Cottage
locations.short_desc: Open May through October, when it is absolutely necessary.
locations.long_desc: The Ice Cream Cottage is a cheerful yellow building on Lake Street that closes every November 1st and reopens every May 1st, a ritual the town marks with a small ceremony. It serves 24 flavors in summer, 12 in shoulder seasons. The signature flavor is Brindlewick Maple, which uses local syrup and is the color of autumn.

seasonal_variations.spring: The cottage reopened two weeks ago. The line isn't long yet.
seasonal_variations.summer: The line goes around the building on hot days. Worth it.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Apple cider soft-serve is the seasonal special. The season ends October 31.
seasonal_variations.winter: Closed for winter. A hand-lettered sign: "See you in April."

time_variations.morning: Closed. The ice cream starts at 11.
time_variations.afternoon: Busiest time. The line moves quickly.
time_variations.evening: Open until 9 in summer. The line is social.
time_variations.night: Closed after 9. The night is warm enough to want ice cream.

locations.name: Meadow's Florist
locations.short_desc: Flowers, dried arrangements, and window boxes — run by Beatrice Meadow, who grows most of what she sells.
locations.long_desc: The florist is a bright, fragrant shop on Millpond Row. Beatrice Meadow grows a substantial portion of her stock in the field behind her house on Birch Hollow Road, supplementing with the greenhouse and local farms. She has done the flowers for every wedding in Brindlewick for 28 years.

seasonal_variations.spring: The shop is overwhelmed with spring stock — tulips, lilies, peonies.
seasonal_variations.summer: Sunflowers and zinnias. The cooler is packed.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Dahlias and chrysanthemums. Dried arrangements in the window.
seasonal_variations.winter: Poinsettias and forced paperwhites. The shop smells of pine resin and eucalyptus.

time_variations.morning: The freshest flowers at opening time.
time_variations.afternoon: Arrangements being made. The cooler door opens and closes.
time_variations.evening: Closing up. The end-of-day flowers sometimes discounted.
time_variations.night: Dark. The cooler hums inside.

locations.name: Brindlewick Savings
locations.short_desc: A small independent bank in a handsome 1920s building — the same family has run it for four generations.
locations.long_desc: The bank occupies a well-preserved 1921 building with a proper vault door visible through the glass partition. The Hartwell-adjacent family that runs it (the Morrows) have been here since 1921. It is not a national bank; it is not affiliated with any chain. It is the Brindlewick Savings Bank, and the fact that it survived every financial crisis is a point of deep local pride.

seasonal_variations.spring: The window display has something to do with spring financing. You choose not to look closely.
seasonal_variations.summer: The lobby AC is the best in town. People stop in just to cool down.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Harvest lending. A lot of farm accounts being settled.
seasonal_variations.winter: End-of-year business. The lobby has a small decorated tree.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 9. The tellers are efficient.
time_variations.afternoon: Closing at 3 most days. The drive-through is faster.
time_variations.evening: The branch is closed. The ATM outside works.
time_variations.night: Dark. The ATM is lit.

locations.name: The Old Schoolhouse Arts Center
locations.short_desc: The original 1836 schoolhouse, now an arts center for classes and community events.
locations.long_desc: The original schoolhouse — Patience Alderman's room, where she taught every age in 1836 — was preserved and converted into an arts center in 1992. It offers pottery classes (supplementary to the studio), painting, music lessons, and hosts the annual children's art show. The slate board from 1836 is still on the wall, now protected behind glass, still showing a long division problem no one has thought to erase.

seasonal_variations.spring: A student show on the walls. Bright, earnest work. The whole building smells of fresh paint.
seasonal_variations.summer: Summer workshops for children. The parking lot is full of minivans.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The arts center's main season. Exhibitions, talks, a poetry series on Fridays.
seasonal_variations.winter: Holiday gift show in December. Local artists selling work out of the main room.

time_variations.morning: Classes start at 10. The smell of paint is always present.
time_variations.afternoon: Studio time. The afternoon light is good.
time_variations.evening: Evening classes and openings.
time_variations.night: Dark. A security light at the door.

locations.name: The Station Master's House
locations.short_desc: The old station master's house at the south end of Lake Street, beside the disused railway platform where the valley line once terminated.
locations.long_desc: The Station Master's House sits at the quiet southern foot of Lake Street, where the old Mirrowell Valley Branch Line once arrived from the south. The line closed in 1923; Gerald Hobbs, the last station master, locked up and left the same week. The house has been vacant since. The railway platform beside it is an overgrown concrete slab — you can still make out the painted letters 'BRINDLEWICK' in faded black on the concrete face. And on a nail by the door, as Hobbs left it, is a brass door key on a faded red ribbon, slightly greened with age.

seasonal_variations.spring: The roses along the fence are budding. The house hasn't been occupied in years.
seasonal_variations.summer: The garden has gone to seed. Something is living in the old shed.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The house looks most handsome in October — the color of the climbing vine against the clapboards.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the widow's walk. The house is dark. The tracks long since gone.

time_variations.morning: Quiet. The house has been empty for years.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon light on the east side.
time_variations.evening: The house is dark.
time_variations.night: Very dark. The garden untended.

locations.name: The Hidden Garden
locations.short_desc: A walled garden at the back of the Alderman Estate, full of flowers that shouldn't survive this climate.
locations.long_desc: The walled garden at the back of the Alderman Estate is hidden by an overgrown hawthorn hedge. Inside: a formal garden in full bloom, with species that have no business flowering this far north — a southern magnolia, a fig tree trained against the south wall, lavender in abundance. It is warm in here in a way that seems disproportionate. Constance Alderman says the south-facing wall and the old brick create a microclimate. She's right, but it's still remarkable. This is where she comes when she needs to think.

seasonal_variations.spring: The garden has woken up. Snowdrops first, then everything else in a rush.
seasonal_variations.summer: In full bloom. Whoever planted this knew what they were doing.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Late dahlias and sedums. The garden is still beautiful in its decay.
seasonal_variations.winter: The garden is a study in structure — bare stems and dried seed heads under snow.

time_variations.morning: The garden catches the morning sun first.
time_variations.afternoon: The shade from the old wall makes the afternoon pleasant.
time_variations.evening: The garden is quiet in the evening. The smell intensifies.
time_variations.night: Dark. The flowers are invisible but the smell remains.

locations.name: Finch Lane
locations.short_desc: The oldest residential street in Brindlewick — narrow, tree-lined, with front porches and window boxes.
locations.long_desc: Finch Lane runs parallel to the lake one block back, lined with old houses, most dating from the 1860s-1890s. The trees here are the tallest in town, maples and elms that arch over the road. Most houses have porches. People sit on them. Conversations happen across fences. It is the street where Eleanor grew up, where Rosalind Webb's shop has been for three generations, and where the Alderman Estate occupies its large corner lot.

seasonal_variations.spring: Lilac bushes on both sides of the lane are about to bloom.
seasonal_variations.summer: Shaded and pleasant. The lane smells of warm asphalt and cut grass.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Leaves from the old maples cover the lane completely.
seasonal_variations.winter: Snow on the lane. Well-shoveled by the residents here.

time_variations.morning: A quiet lane in the morning. A good cut-through.
time_variations.afternoon: The lane is dappled in afternoon.
time_variations.evening: Pleasant in the evening.
time_variations.night: One streetlight at the corner.

locations.name: Maple Row
locations.short_desc: The main residential and civic street — school, chapel, cemetery, doctor, and a long row of houses.
locations.long_desc: Maple Row is where most of Brindlewick's civic institutions cluster: the elementary school, the chapel, the old schoolhouse arts center, the community hall, and the doctor's practice. It's also a residential street, running from the north edge of town south to the town square area. The row of maples it's named for are gone (removed in 1988 after disease) but a replanting program in 2005 has replaced them with young maples that are beginning to look proper.

seasonal_variations.spring: The maples are budding. In two weeks this street will be green-canopied.
seasonal_variations.summer: A tunnel of green. The most beautiful block in town.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Maple Row lives up to its name in October. The color is unreasonable.
seasonal_variations.winter: The bare maples make a cathedral of the street. Quite beautiful.

time_variations.morning: The street is quiet until school starts.
time_variations.afternoon: Children on the sidewalk after school.
time_variations.evening: People walking dogs in the evening.
time_variations.night: The street lights on. Very quiet.

locations.name: Lake Street
locations.short_desc: The street between town and water — the inn, the pub, and the smell of the lake at every turn.
locations.long_desc: Lake Street runs along the inland side of the lakefront, between the town's main streets and the park. The inn and pub anchor its northern end. Going south, houses with lake views, the fire station, the ice cream cottage, and finally the path to the boardwalk. The smell of the lake is always present here — clean and cold in winter, warm and slightly weedy in August.

seasonal_variations.spring: Lake Street is fully alive again. The ice cream cottage is open.
seasonal_variations.summer: The commercial heart of the lakefront. Busy all day.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Quieter. The tourist shops are closing up for the season.
seasonal_variations.winter: Most shops shuttered. The street is quiet and snow-covered.

time_variations.morning: Lake Street wakes up with the boat rental traffic.
time_variations.afternoon: Busiest in the afternoon.
time_variations.evening: The boardwalk draws people in the evening.
time_variations.night: Quiet after 9.

locations.name: Millpond Row
locations.short_desc: The commercial main street — bakery, general store, diner, library, post office, and most things you need.
locations.long_desc: Millpond Row is the town's commercial spine — a street of modest Victorian and early 20th century buildings in good repair, their facades varied but harmonious. The bakery smell from the Copper Kettle carries the length of the street in the morning. The library's reading room windows glow on winter afternoons. The notice board in front of Hartwell's General Store is the town's most-read publication.
