seasonal_variations.spring: The row is pleasant in spring. The millpond smell drifts up on warm days.
seasonal_variations.summer: Bakery smell in the morning, diner smell in the afternoon. A comfortable street.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The trees on the millpond end have turned. A good corridor of color.
seasonal_variations.winter: The millpond is frozen. The row is well-plowed. The bakery light is on before dawn.

time_variations.morning: Bakery customers cut through the row in the morning.
time_variations.afternoon: The diner does its lunch business here.
time_variations.evening: The diner keeps the row lit until 9.
time_variations.night: Dark. The millpond nearby.

locations.name: Finch Island
locations.short_desc: The larger of Lake Mirrowell's two islands — wooded, accessible by rowboat, with the ruins of a summer camp.
locations.long_desc: Finch Island is about three acres of mixed woodland, accessible only by boat. A footpath circles it in about twenty minutes. At the south end, the ruins of the Old Sycamore Camp (a 1930s-60s summer camp) — a few cabin foundations, a firepit, an overgrown playing field. The island is home to herons, which nest in the tall pines at the north end. No one lives here. The island is public land.

seasonal_variations.spring: The island is inaccessible — the ice is gone but the dock needs repair.
seasonal_variations.summer: The island is worth the row across. Herons nest in the pines on the north end.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The maple on the south shore turns orange two weeks before the mainland trees.
seasonal_variations.winter: You can walk across on the ice when it's thick enough. Not now — maybe February.

time_variations.morning: The herons are still on the north shore in the morning.
time_variations.afternoon: The island is warm in the afternoon sun.
time_variations.evening: The best view of the sunset from the island's west side.
time_variations.night: The island is very dark. The loons call across the water.

locations.name: Spruce Point
locations.short_desc: A wooded headland on the lake's eastern shore, accessible by trail — a good view of the western mountains and the town.
locations.long_desc: Spruce Point is a twenty-minute walk east along the lake shore path. The trail is pleasant; the destination is a rocky headland where spruce trees grow at the water's edge. Looking back west, you see the town of Brindlewick reflected in the lake, and in the middle distance, the lighthouse. If the lake light is going to be visible, it's visible from here — and the angle suggests it comes from the deepest section of the lake, due south of the lighthouse.
locations.address: Spruce Point, Lake Mirrowell East Shore

seasonal_variations.spring: The point emerges from snowmelt. Good fishing as the lake warms.
seasonal_variations.summer: Locals swim from the point in the evenings. The water is clear here.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Spectacular views of the foliage across the lake. Cold and worth it.
seasonal_variations.winter: Wind-exposed in winter. The lake ice cracks here in cold snaps. Eerie sound.

time_variations.morning: The fishermen are here before anyone else.
time_variations.afternoon: Swimmers in summer. The water is warmest here.
time_variations.evening: The point is beautiful at dusk.
time_variations.night: The lake sounds carry across the water.

locations.name: Brindlewick Candle & Soap
locations.short_desc: Handmade candles and soap, in a shop that smells almost impossibly good.
locations.long_desc: Flora Kincaid has been making candles and soap in her basement and selling them from this shop since 2003. The scents are all locally inspired: Lake Mist, Apple Orchard, Cedar and Snow, Woodsmoke. The shop is narrow and warm and very fragrant. Flora knows more about botany than her occupation suggests.

seasonal_variations.spring: Beeswax candles and lavender soap. The spring scent collection is out.
seasonal_variations.summer: Citronella and lemon. Batches of herbal soap curing on the back shelves.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Cinnamon and spice. The shop is doing its best impression of a bakery.
seasonal_variations.winter: Balsam and cedar. Holiday gift sets in the window. The shop is very good at this.

time_variations.morning: Open at 10. The whole street smells of it on warm mornings.
time_variations.afternoon: Quiet weekday afternoons. A good time to browse.
time_variations.evening: Closing. The candles in the window still lit.
time_variations.night: Dark. The candles out.

locations.name: The Notions Nook
locations.short_desc: Fabric, thread, buttons, and all the materials needed to make or repair soft things.
locations.long_desc: The Notions Nook has been on Millpond Row since 1967 and has always been run by a member of the Choi family — currently Min-Ji Choi, 48. It sells fabric by the yard, patterns, thread, buttons, zippers, ribbons, and everything else the crafty or the practical-minded require. Min-Ji also does alterations and can fix almost anything made of cloth.

seasonal_variations.spring: Spring sewing fabrics in the window. Pastel cotton, light linen.
seasonal_variations.summer: Quilting season. The back room is full of groups working on projects.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Flannel and felt. Halloween and harvest patterns.
seasonal_variations.winter: The Christmas fabric display is ambitious. Everyone is making something for someone.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 10. Bolts of fabric near the door.
time_variations.afternoon: Quilters in the back room on Saturdays.
time_variations.evening: Closing at 5.
time_variations.night: Dark.

locations.name: Thornbury's Cheese & Provisions
locations.short_desc: Cheese, charcuterie, local preserves, and the occasional thing you'd only find here.
locations.long_desc: Thornbury's is a specialty food shop run by Edna Thornbury, 62 — cheese from local farms and further afield, cured meats, pickles, jams, local maple syrup, the Perkins cider by the bottle. It is the shop you visit when you want something particular.

seasonal_variations.spring: Spring cheese varieties in the case. Ramps from the mountain, if you ask.
seasonal_variations.summer: The charcuterie case is deep. Local honey, summer sausage, pickles.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Harvest cheeses. Aged cheddar. Preserves and jams from local farms.
seasonal_variations.winter: The best place to assemble a holiday gift basket. Aged cheeses and imported things.

time_variations.morning: The cheese case is restocked in the morning.
time_variations.afternoon: Lunch hours are busy.
time_variations.evening: Closing. Pre-made platters available.
time_variations.night: Dark. The cooler running.

locations.name: The Cobbler's Corner
locations.short_desc: Shoe and leather repair, run by Parveen Nair, who can make a worn pair of boots last another decade.
locations.long_desc: The cobbler's shop is small and practical, smelling of leather and saddle soap. Parveen Nair, 44, moved to Brindlewick in 2012 from the city, drawn by, she says, 'the pace.' She repairs shoes, bags, belts, and has taken up saddlery for the small local equestrian community.

seasonal_variations.spring: Spring cleaning for boots. Half the town's shoes are in here.
seasonal_variations.summer: Sandal repairs, mostly. A slower season.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Boot resoling season. He's backed up through November.
seasonal_variations.winter: Winter boot repairs, waterproofing, new soles. The smell of leather and wax.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 9. The repairs from yesterday are ready.
time_variations.afternoon: The cobbler works continuously. The shop smells of leather.
time_variations.evening: Closing. A handwritten receipt for your ticket.
time_variations.night: Dark.

locations.name: The Keeper's Cottage
locations.short_desc: The small cottage behind the library where Eleanor and Harold Finch-Hartwell have lived since 1985.
locations.long_desc: The cottage is connected to the library property by a garden gate. It is small and full of books, naturally. Harold's vegetable garden takes up most of the back yard. Eleanor's study light can be seen from Millpond Row, often still on at midnight, which the neighbors have given up commenting on.

seasonal_variations.spring: The keeper's garden has come back. Window boxes freshly filled.
seasonal_variations.summer: Geraniums in the windows. The lighthouse beam is visible from here on foggy nights.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The cottage is stocked for winter. Woodpile built high along the south wall.
seasonal_variations.winter: Smoke from the chimney. The only warm light for half a mile.

time_variations.morning: The keeper is an early riser. Coffee on by 6.
time_variations.afternoon: Quiet. The keeper is often at the lighthouse.
time_variations.evening: The cottage is lit and warm by evening.
time_variations.night: A lamp in the window. The lighthouse beam is visible from here.

locations.name: Millbrook Farm
locations.short_desc: A working farm on the north edge of town — vegetables, chickens, and Fletcher Grange talking to his crops.
locations.long_desc: Millbrook Farm is a 40-acre working farm that has been in some kind of cultivation since the 1840s. Fletcher Grange, 61, runs it now — mixed vegetables, chickens, a small beef herd. He is not technically of the Grange organization but serves on their board out of a sense of nominal obligation. He talks to his crops. He maintains this is agronomically sensible and quotes studies. The results suggest he may be right.

seasonal_variations.spring: The farm is in motion — animals out again, fields being turned, the smell of fresh soil.
seasonal_variations.summer: Full production. Vegetable rows, chickens, the sound of machinery.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Harvest. Trucks in and out. The barn smell of hay and cold earth.
seasonal_variations.winter: The farm is quieter but not still. Animals to tend, equipment to maintain.

time_variations.morning: The farm starts before the town wakes up.
time_variations.afternoon: The afternoon is the quietest part of the farm day.
time_variations.evening: Animals put up. The farm is settling.
time_variations.night: Dark and still. The stars are exceptional from here.

locations.name: The Old Meeting House
locations.short_desc: The original 1838 Quaker meeting house, now a small local history museum open on weekends.
locations.long_desc: The meeting house predates the chapel by several years and was built by a handful of Quaker families among the early settlers. It became a general community meeting space in the 1860s when the Quaker families moved on, and was converted to a small museum in 1987. It holds artifacts from the town's first fifty years: tools, clothing, documents, a remarkably good collection of early maps.

seasonal_variations.spring: The museum has been cleaned for the season. New interpretive signs.
seasonal_variations.summer: Tour groups from the historic register. Docent on Saturdays.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The meeting house is especially atmospheric in October — bare trees, cold light.
seasonal_variations.winter: Closed for the season except by appointment.

time_variations.morning: Opens at 10. The docent arrives at 9:45.
time_variations.afternoon: Tours on the hour.
time_variations.evening: Closed by 4.
time_variations.night: Dark and locked.

locations.name: The Old Ice House
locations.short_desc: A mid-19th century ice storage building near the boardwalk — unused, but still standing, still cold inside.
locations.long_desc: The ice house is a thick-walled stone building near the old boathouse, used until the 1940s for storing lake ice cut in winter. It's on the town's historic register and therefore can't be demolished. It's locked, but the stone keeps it noticeably cooler than the outside air even in summer, which you can feel near its walls. Inside (visible through a high small window): sawdust on the floor, the memory of cold.

seasonal_variations.spring: The last of the winter's ice is still packed inside, sawdust-covered, slowly melting.
seasonal_variations.summer: The thick walls keep it cold even in August. The ice lasted into July this year.
seasonal_variations.autumn: Empty now. The smell of wet sawdust and old cold.
seasonal_variations.winter: Ice cutting will start in January if the lake is thick enough.

time_variations.morning: The cold strikes you as soon as you step in.
time_variations.afternoon: The same cold, every hour.
time_variations.evening: Dark inside regardless of outside light.
time_variations.night: Very dark. Very cold.

locations.name: The Warming Hut
locations.short_desc: A small shelter on the trail system, with a wood stove and a supply of emergency firewood.
locations.long_desc: The warming hut is a three-sided shelter with a wood stove at the trail junction point, about 1.2 miles up from the trailhead. It's maintained by the volunteer fire department and Clem Rourke. A supply of firewood is kept under a tarpaulin outside. Inside: a bench along each wall, a rudimentary first aid kit, and a visitor log dating back to 1985. Some entries are brief ('Cold. 2 miles in. Home for pie.'). Some are longer.

seasonal_variations.spring: The warming hut is closed until next winter. A padlock on the door.
seasonal_variations.summer: Unused. The wood stove sits cold. A good place to shelter in a storm.
seasonal_variations.autumn: The hut will open in November. Someone has been in to sweep and stack wood.
seasonal_variations.winter: Open and warm. The trail register sits on the windowsill. The stove takes the edge off.

time_variations.morning: The stove is usually warm in winter — someone tends it for early hikers.
time_variations.afternoon: A good midday stop. The log bench is worn smooth.
time_variations.evening: Quiet. The stove still going if someone was in today.
time_variations.night: Dark. The stove banked but warm.

locations.name: The Farmers' Market
locations.short_desc: Saturday morning, June through October — tables and tents in the town square, everyone selling something.
locations.long_desc: The farmers' market happens every Saturday from the first Saturday in June to the last in October. It fills the town square with tents: produce from Millbrook Farm and the Finch orchard, Perkins cider, Meadow's flowers, Sylvie Tran's pottery, Margaret Hollis's knitwear, someone's jam, someone else's honey. The Copper Kettle sells out of whatever Mari brings within the first hour.

time_variations.morning: The market is in full swing — peak hour is 9 to 11.
time_variations.afternoon: Vendors are packing up by 1.
